title: Moules marinière
subtitle: Mussels steamed in white wine, shallot, and parsley.
excerpt: A kilo of fresh mussels opens in five minutes over a base of soft shallot, garlic, and white wine. Crusty bread for the broth. The classic Breton bowl that crosses every French and Belgian café.
sourceNotes: Standard French marinière technique. Mussel-safety guidance follows long-established food-safety convention: tightly closed before, properly open after.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mussels are best cooked and eaten the same day. The shallot-wine base can be softened up to an hour ahead and reheated.

glossaryTerms.definition: Remove the wiry byssal threads — the beard — by which a mussel anchors itself to a rock or rope. Pinch and pull firmly downwards towards the hinge.

content.text: A kilo of mussels for two people, a glass of white wine, a shallot softened in butter. The whole thing takes ten minutes once the mussels are cleaned. Bread on the side to soak up the broth, and a glass of the same wine you cooked in.
The two rules are these. Before cooking, throw away any mussel that stays open when you tap it on the worktop. After cooking, throw away any that stayed shut. Everything else is generous.

items.prepNote: fresh, in shell, scrubbed and debearded

content.text: You need a tall pot with a tight lid: a 6 to 8 litre stockpot is right for one kilo of mussels. They expand as they open.

Tip {{mussels}} of mussels into a sink of cold water. Scrub each shell with a kitchen brush, pull off the beard, and discard any that are cracked. Tap any that are open against the worktop. If they don't close within a few seconds, throw them away. Drain in a colander.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in the stockpot over medium heat. Add {{shallot}} of chopped shallots and a pinch of salt. Cook for 4 minutes until soft and translucent, not coloured. Stir in {{garlic}} of chopped garlic for the last 30 seconds.

Turn the heat up high. Pour in {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine and bring to a fast boil. Tip in the mussels all at once and clamp on the lid. Cook for 3 to 4 minutes, shaking the pot vigorously once halfway through, until all the shells have opened. Stir in {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. If using the cream, pour it in at the end and swirl through.
Tip into two warmed bowls along with all the broth. Discard any mussels that have stayed shut. Serve immediately with chips, crusty bread, or both.

items.cause: Not soaked or scrubbed long enough.
items.fix: Soak in cold water for 20 minutes before cleaning. Scrub each shell well and rinse in fresh water before they go in the pot.

items.symptom: Some mussels stayed shut after cooking
items.cause: They were dead before going in.
items.fix: Throw away any unopened ones after cooking. They are not safe to eat.

items.cause: Too much wine for the amount of mussels.
items.fix: Lift the cooked mussels out into bowls, then reduce the broth at high heat for 1 to 2 minutes before pouring over.

items.cause: Left in the pot too long after opening.
items.fix: As soon as most shells are open, kill the heat. They keep cooking in the hot broth.

content.text: Moules à la crème adds the optional double cream at the end. Moules au cidre swaps the white wine for dry Breton cider and works particularly well with a small handful of crisp diced apple. For a Provençal version, soften the shallots with a fennel seed and a tomato chopped fine; finish with a pinch of saffron.

In Brittany this is what you order at any harbour-side café in Cancale or Saint-Malo, where the mussel-ropes hang in the bay outside the window. In Belgium it pairs with frites and a glass of trappist beer. The French version stays close to the white wine, the shallot, and the parsley: four ingredients, eight minutes, and a piece of bread to wipe the bowl.
